planting.note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; perennial basil benefits from regular tip-harvesting to prevent flowering, which prolongs the productive leafy growth stage.

directions: Root stem cuttings in water or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; perennial basil (African basil, East Indian basil, spice basil) is a tropical perennial species grown in Gulf Coast and subtropical gardens for its superior performance in the heat and humidity that causes standard sweet basil (O. basilicum) to bolt and decline rapidly in midsummer — perennial basil withstands zone 9–12 summer heat, continues producing through the hottest months, and in frost-free climates grows as a large, multi-stemmed perennial shrub to 3–4 feet that persists for several years. The flavor is similar to sweet basil but carries an additional spicy, clove-like note that distinguishes it in cooking. Like all basils, the culinary quality is best before flowering; tip-prune regularly to delay bolting. It roots readily from stem cuttings placed in water, making it easy to propagate and share. Excellent in containers and mixed herb borders as a large, bushy accent. In zones below 9, grow as a long-season annual or bring cuttings indoors in fall to overwinter.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile potting mix or garden soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; lemon geranium prefers slightly drier conditions than many herbs — allow the soil to dry moderately between waterings to avoid root rot.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in partial shade to full sun; lemon geranium is a South African scented-leaf pelargonium with intensely lemon-scented, small, crinkled, fan-shaped leaves that release a remarkably true, clean lemon fragrance — not the synthetic-citrus scent of lemon verbena but a pure, fresh, clear lemon aroma that is one of the finest natural citrus scents available in an herb garden plant. The leaves are used to flavor sugar, baked goods, tea, jellies, and custards; placed in the bottom of a greased cake pan before adding batter, they impart a delicate lemon perfume to pound cake and sponge cakes. Small, pale-pink to lavender flowers appear at intervals. Unlike most pelargoniums, lemon geranium tolerates partial shade acceptably, though it flowers most heavily in full sun. In zones 9–11 it grows as a perennial sub-shrub; elsewhere it is grown as a container plant brought indoors in autumn. 'Prince Rupert' (upright, strong-growing), 'French Lace' (compact, cream-variegated leaves), and 'Cy's Sunburst' are notable cultivars.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade; peppermint geranium is the most shade-tolerant of the scented pelargoniums and performs acceptably in quite bright indirect light — avoid hot, intense afternoon sun which causes the velvety leaves to scorch.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average soil in partial shade; peppermint geranium is the most shade-tolerant and large-leaved of the scented pelargoniums — its enormous, densely hairy, velvety, gray-green, rounded leaves give the plant a soft, textural, spreading quality unlike any other herb, and crush the leaves to release an intensely fresh, clear peppermint-eucalyptus fragrance that is one of the most pleasant and cooling herb scents available. The velvety, felt-like texture of the large leaves is as much a tactile as a visual pleasure. It spreads by lax, trailing stems to cover considerable ground in partial shade, making it useful as a low, scented groundcover in shaded containers, window boxes, and garden edges. Small, white, purple-spotted flowers appear at intervals. Leaves are used to flavor sachets, tea, and baked goods. It is more water-tolerant than most scented pelargoniums. The most commonly grown cultivar for ground coverage and shade tolerance is the straight species; 'Chocolate Mint' (dark-spotted center) is an attractive variant.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; aztec sweet herb spreads by rooting stolons and can be used as a low groundcover in tropical gardens — trim periodically to maintain compact growth.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average soil in partial shade to full sun; aztec sweet herb (hierba dulce, Mexican candy herb, honeyherb) is a Mexican native tropical herb that contains hernandulcin, a natural sesquiterpene sweetener approximately 1,000 times sweeter than sucrose by weight — placing a single small leaf on the tongue immediately produces an intensely sweet, slightly anise-flavored sweetness that lasts for several minutes. The plant has been used in traditional Mexican medicine and as a sweetening herb since pre-Columbian times. It grows as a low, spreading, stoloniferous herb with small, oval, hairy, wrinkled leaves and small white flower clusters, and functions as a low groundcover in tropical garden settings. Current medical interest focuses on hernandulcin's potential as a natural low-calorie sweetener. Tea made from the fresh or dried leaves is pleasantly and intensely sweet with subtle herbal notes. In zones 9–12 it grows as a perennial groundcover; in cooler climates it is grown in containers brought indoors in autumn.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in spring or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun to partial shade; red valerian self-seeds prolifically and thrives in poor, dry, alkaline conditions — rich, fertile, or wet soil produces rank, floppy growth and reduces flowering.

directions: Direct-sow or plant container specimens in well-drained to dry, poor to average, slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; red valerian (Jupiter's beard, fox's brush, kiss-me-quick) is a Mediterranean perennial that has naturalized on rocky coastal cliffs, stone walls, and dry limestone outcrops throughout Europe and warm-climate North America — its ability to root into the smallest crevices of dry stone walls and cliff faces, producing clouds of intensely fragrant, deep coral-red, rosy-pink, or white flower clusters from late spring through summer, makes it a classic companion to stone walls, gravel gardens, and dry, chalky slopes. The young leaves and roots are edible (leaves used in salads in France and Italy as a raw herb with a mild, slightly bitter flavor), and the large, fluffy seed heads provide excellent cut material for dried arrangements. It self-seeds freely into any available dry, alkaline crack or gravel area, naturalizing remarkably quickly. Excellent in dry stone wall gardens, Mediterranean-style plantings, and hot, dry coastal gardens. 'Albus' (white), 'Coccineus' (deep crimson-red), and 'Rosenrot' (rose-pink) are named cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
